concepts.lesson: Land revenue was the tax that peasants had to pay to the government for using agricultural land. During British rule in India, land revenue became the main source of income for the colonial administration. The British introduced different systems to collect revenue from farmers. These systems often placed heavy financial pressure on peasants and affected their livelihoods.
concepts.real_example: Farmers paying tax to the British government for the land they cultivated.
key_points: Tax paid by farmers for land
Main income source for the British government

concepts.lesson: The Permanent Settlement was a land revenue system introduced by the British in 1793 by Lord Cornwallis. Under this system, zamindars were made the owners of the land and were responsible for collecting revenue from peasants. The amount of revenue to be paid to the British government was fixed permanently. Zamindars had to pay the fixed revenue on time, otherwise their land could be taken away.
concepts.real_example: Zamindars collecting taxes from peasants and paying a fixed amount to the British government.

concepts.lesson: Zamindars were landholders who collected land revenue from peasants on behalf of the British government. After the Permanent Settlement, they became the owners of large areas of land. They collected taxes from peasants and paid a fixed amount to the government. If zamindars failed to pay the required revenue, their lands could be auctioned.
concepts.real_example: A zamindar collecting tax from farmers in his village.
key_points: Paid fixed tax to the government

concepts.lesson: Peasants were farmers who worked on the land to grow crops. Under the British revenue systems, peasants had to pay high taxes and often borrowed money from moneylenders to pay their dues. Many peasants faced poverty and hardship due to heavy taxation and unfair farming practices.
concepts.real_example: Farmers cultivating crops like rice or wheat and paying taxes to the zamindar.
key_points: Had to pay heavy land taxes

concepts.lesson: Indigo was a plant used to produce blue dye for colouring cloth. During British rule, European planters forced Indian peasants to grow indigo instead of food crops. Peasants were often given advance loans and then forced to sell the indigo at very low prices. This system caused great hardship to farmers.
concepts.real_example: Farmers growing indigo plants to produce blue dye for export to Europe.
key_points: Indigo used to make blue dye
Sold at low prices to planters

concepts.lesson: Indigo planters were European landowners who controlled indigo production in India. They forced peasants to grow indigo on their land and provided loans to farmers in advance. However, peasants often received very little profit and were trapped in debt because they had to sell indigo at very low prices.
concepts.real_example: European planters managing indigo farms in Bengal.

concepts.lesson: The Indigo Revolt was a protest by peasants against indigo planters in Bengal in 1859–1860. Farmers refused to grow indigo because it was unprofitable and caused them hardship. The revolt spread across many villages, and peasants demanded fair treatment. As a result of the protests, the British government appointed a commission to investigate the issue.
concepts.real_example: Peasants in Bengal refusing to grow indigo crops for European planters.
key_points: Led to investigation by the British government
